verdict_reasoning: LYN is a Src-family tyrosine kinase with documented dual localization. It is primarily cytoplasmic and involved in intracellular signal transduction (particularly in immune cells like mast cells and B cells), but possesses an N-terminal myristoylation site that anchors it to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane and intracellular membranes. Critically, LYN is also recruited to the outer leaflet via stable non-covalent association with membrane-proximal domains of surface receptors (BCR, FcεRI, other immune receptors) during signal initiation—a well-documented mechanism in B-cell and mast-cell biology. This receptor-associated pool constitutes a functionally important surface-proximal population. Additionally, some Src-family kinases reach the outer leaflet transiently or constitutively via lipid-binding or complex assembly with surface partners. The protein thus qualifies as contextual via dual_localization (inner-leaflet myristoylation + receptor-associated surface-proximal signaling complex) rather than classical surface accessibility.
